# Writing models in BUGS/JAGS syntax

So far the computer has done the writing for you. Now you take over. FANGS reads
models written in the **BUGS/JAGS language** — a compact way of describing a model
that reads almost exactly like the maths you would scribble on paper. You state
how the data and parameters relate, and the sampler works out how to fit it.

## The building blocks

There are really only four pieces to learn:

- **Stochastic nodes** use `~`, read aloud as *"is distributed as"*. This is how
  you say a quantity is random:
  `jaw[i] ~ dnorm(mu, sigma)`
- **Deterministic nodes** use `<-`, read as *"is calculated as"*. This is for
  quantities you compute exactly from others:
  `mu[i] <- alpha + beta * body[i]`
- **Loops** repeat a statement over every fish in the dataset:
  `for (i in 1:N) { ... }`
- **Priors** are just stochastic nodes placed on the parameters themselves:
  `alpha ~ dnorm(0, 5)`

That is the whole grammar. Everything else is built from these four.

## A minimal frogfish model

Here is the smallest useful model: the jaw lengths of one species scatter around a
common mean `mu`, with spread `sigma`.

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    length[i] ~ dnorm(mu, sigma)
  }
  mu ~ dnorm(40, 20)
  sigma ~ dunif(0, 50)
}
```

Read it top to bottom: *every measured `length` is normally distributed around a shared `mu`; our prior belief is that `mu` sits somewhere around 40 mm; and the spread `sigma` is somewhere between 0 and 50 mm.*

## The one thing that trips everyone up

FANGS describes the normal distribution by its **standard deviation**, not its
precision: `dnorm(mean, SD)`. Most textbooks and classic JAGS/BUGS code instead
use precision, $\tau = 1/\sigma^2$. So if you copy a model from a textbook, you
must convert: $\sigma = 1/\sqrt{\tau}$. Forgetting this is the single most common
source of "why is my fit nonsense?" — so it is worth burning into memory now.

## Your task

The model in the challenge has a deliberate **syntax error**. Fix it so the parser
accepts it. The error message points you to the exact line and column — let it do
the work.
